mnemonics.title: FLIP for 1's Complement
mnemonics.content: FLIP = Flip All LSBs and Preserve sign. In 1's complement, simply flip (invert) each bit: 0→1, 1→0. The leftmost bit is the sign bit (0=positive, 1=negative).

mnemonics.title: Add One More (AOM) for 2's Complement
mnemonics.content: AOM = Add One More to 1's complement. To get 2's complement: first find 1's complement, then add 1 to the result. This is how computers represent negative numbers.

mnemonics.title: 9-9-9 Subtract for 9's Complement
mnemonics.content: 9's Complement = 99...9 minus each digit. For each digit in the number, subtract it from 9. Example: 456 → 543 (9-4=5, 9-5=4, 9-6=3).

mnemonics.title: Ten Makes Perfect (TMP) for 10's Complement
mnemonics.content: TMP = Ten Makes Perfect. First find 9's complement, then add 1. Or simply: subtract each digit from 10, handle borrowing like normal subtraction from zero.

mnemonics.title: MSB Tells Sign in Binary
mnemonics.content: MSB (Most Significant Bit) = Sign Bit. In signed binary with complements: MSB=0 means positive (use value directly), MSB=1 means negative (use complement to find magnitude).

mnemonics.title: Subtract with ADD using Complements
mnemonics.content: To subtract A-B using complements: Add A + (complement of B). If there's an end carry, add it back (for diminished radix) or discard (for radix complement).

acronyms.usage: The leftmost bit in a binary number. In complement representation, MSB is the sign bit (0=positive, 1=negative).
The rightmost bit in a binary number. This is where you add 1 when computing 2's complement.
The base of the number system. Binary has radix 2, Decimal has radix 10. Complements are defined relative to the radix.
The true complement (2's complement for binary, 10's complement for decimal). Formula: R^n - N where n is number of digits.
One less than the radix complement (1's complement for binary, 9's complement for decimal). Formula: (R^n - 1) - N.
Mnemonic for subtraction using complements: Add the number to the complement of subtrahend. For radix complement, discard carry; for DRC, add carry back.

visualTips.visualization: Picture a number line with 0 in center. 1's complement is like reflecting across the middle point. 2's complement is like reflecting and taking one step forward. Think of 2's complement as the 'mirror plus one step' - the mirror gives you 1's complement, the step adds 1.

Imagine a circular dial with digits 0-9. 9's complement is like looking at the mirror image of your number. 10's complement is the mirror image plus moving one position clockwise. The circle helps visualize why 9's+1=10's complement.

Picture a circle with 0000 at top, counting clockwise. 2's complement shows symmetry: +7 (0111) to -1 (1111) wrap around. The overflow detection becomes obvious - crossing from positive to negative side of the circle incorrectly means overflow.
For n bits: 1's complement range is -(2^(n-1)-1) to +(2^(n-1)-1). 2's complement range is -2^(n-1) to +(2^(n-1)-1). Draw as a number wheel where negative numbers wrap around from the bottom.

Traditional subtraction is like climbing down a ladder (borrowing is hard work). Complement subtraction is like going up the ladder and counting steps - just add! The carry flag tells you if you 'crossed the origin' (meaning no borrow needed).

analogies.analogy: 1's complement is like a photo negative of an image. Every white pixel becomes black and vice versa. Just as a negative 'inverts' colors, 1's complement inverts all bits (0↔1). The original and its negative represent opposite values in signed arithmetic.

2's complement works like a 24-hour clock going past midnight. If it's 23:00 (represented as -1 in 4-bit) and you add 1 hour, you get 24:00 which wraps to 0:00 (discard the carry). This explains why 1111 (-1) + 0001 (1) = 0000 with carry - perfect for arithmetic!

9's complement is like standing between two parallel mirrors. Your reflection appears reversed (like subtracting from 9). The 10's complement is that reflection plus your actual self appearing - you subtract from 10 (the 'whole' of one hand more than 9).

Traditional subtraction with borrowing is like trying to climb down a steep cliff - hard work! Complement subtraction is like taking the elevator to the top and coming down instead. You 'add your way to subtract' - much easier for computers (which only know how to add).

analogies.concept: Signed Number Representation - Two's Complement Hotel
analogies.analogy: Imagine a hotel with floor numbers going positive (0 to 7 on 3rd floor) and negative (-1 to -8 in the basement). The elevator (2's complement) lets you seamlessly go from +7 to -1 by 'wrapping around'. The MSB tells you which floor (positive or negative floors). Overflow happens when you try to go beyond the top or bottom floor.
